topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom air hummed with the orchestrated chaos of a hundred happy conversations. I was nursing a lukewarm champagne flute, trying to appear engaged in a discussion about floral arrangements, when a familiar silhouette caught my eye near the buffet table. It was Sarah. My ex. She was laughing, her dress shimmering under the crystal chandeliers, and she looked… radiant. My fork froze halfway to my mouth. My hand, which had been gripping the glass tightly, suddenly felt strangely numb. I watched as she turned, her eyes sweeping the room, and for a split second, they landed on mine. A strange tightness constricted my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I quickly looked down at my plate, focusing intently on the arrangement of canapés, trying to recalibrate my entire internal compass.

[story 2]

“Oh, look who it is!” Maya’s voice boomed, drawing my attention away from the dance floor. I followed her gaze and saw him—Mark. He was standing near the entrance, talking animatedly with the groom. It was him. My former significant other. He offered a polite, easy smile, the kind that used to melt my defenses. But as he approached, the easy warmth evaporated, replaced by a sudden, jarring coldness that seemed to radiate from his posture. I felt an involuntary tightening in my jaw. I managed a tight nod, my gaze fixed on a distant painting, acutely aware of the way my fingers were curling into a fist beneath the tablecloth. Every nerve ending seemed suddenly hyper-aware of the proximity of this ghost from my past.

[story 3]

The music shifted to a slower melody, and I found myself standing near the edge of the patio, pretending to admire the sunset. Then, there she was. Across the lawn, bathed in the golden light, was Chloe. She was wearing something elegant and utterly captivating. My entire body seemed to lock up. I took a slow, shallow breath, the air suddenly feeling too thin in my lungs. I watched her interact with another guest, and a wave of disorientation washed over me. Why was my stomach doing that strange, churning flip? I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, a nervous habit I hadn't realized I was indulging in. I wanted desperately to melt into the shadows, to become invisible, but I remained rooted to the spot, observing the scene with an unsettling stillness.
